name: 90/90 Diaphragmatic Breathing
primary_cues: Breathe slowly through the nose if comfortable; exhale fully without straining.
Let the belly and lower ribs expand gently; keep shoulders relaxed.
If dizziness occurs, stop and return to normal breathing.
common_mistakes: Forcing big breaths or bracing hard
progressions: Increase duration to 2 minutes per set
Pair exhale with a gentle pelvic tilt (if comfortable)
dosage_notes: Breathing and relaxation are supportive tools to reduce guarding and build confidence in movement (adjunct, not a cure).
range_of_motion_notes: No forced spinal movement; keep it comfortable.

name: Pelvic Tilt (Supine)
primary_cues: Gently tip the pelvis to flatten the low back toward the floor, then return to neutral.
Keep the movement small and smooth—this is not a maximal ab exercise.
dosage_notes: A gentle motion to reduce stiffness and introduce controlled lumbar movement.

name: Cat-Camel (Spinal Mobility)
primary_cues: Move slowly between rounding and arching your back—stay in a comfortable range.
Let the head and tailbone follow the movement; avoid forcing end ranges.
common_mistakes: Forcing deep arch/round and triggering sharp pain
progressions: Pause 3–4 seconds at each end if comfortable
regressions: Do in standing with hands on a table (less load)
dosage_notes: Common gentle mobility drill used in early phases; prioritize symptom-limited movement.

name: Prone on Elbows (Gentle Extension)
primary_cues: Rest on forearms and let the low back gently extend if it feels good.
Stop if symptoms worsen, especially if leg symptoms increase.
common_mistakes: Holding breath or clenching glutes hard
progressions: Progress to press-ups only if tolerated
regressions: Use pillows under chest for smaller extension
dosage_notes: Some people find gentle extension positions relieving; it is optional and must be symptom-guided.
range_of_motion_notes: Gentle extension only; avoid if it clearly aggravates symptoms.

name: Single Knee-to-Chest (Gentle Flexion)
primary_cues: Bring one knee toward the chest gently; keep the other leg relaxed.
You should feel a mild stretch, not sharp pain.
progressions: Try double knee-to-chest only if comfortable
dosage_notes: Optional mobility drill; choose only if it feels relieving and does not flare symptoms.

name: Open Book (Thoracic Rotation)
primary_cues: Keep knees stacked; rotate through the upper back and ribs.
Move slowly; breathe into the opening.
common_mistakes: Letting knees drift apart and twisting low back
progressions: Increase hold to 3–5s at end range
Add a pillow between knees for comfort
dosage_notes: Helpful for people who feel stiff in the upper back; can reduce perceived back tightness.
range_of_motion_notes: Keep low back calm; rotate gently.

name: Hip Flexor Stretch (Half-Kneeling)
primary_cues: Tuck pelvis slightly and squeeze the glute on the back leg.
Keep torso tall; avoid arching the low back.
progressions: Add overhead reach and slight side bend
dosage_notes: Adjunct mobility. Not essential for everyone; prioritize the movements that help you move comfortably.

name: Hamstring Stretch (Supine, Strap)
primary_cues: Keep knee slightly bent if needed; lift leg until a mild stretch.
Avoid aggressive pulling that reproduces sharp leg symptoms.
progressions: Slow active pulses (very small) if tolerated
dosage_notes: Optional stretch. If symptoms feel nerve-like (burning/tingling), reduce intensity and consider clinician review.
range_of_motion_notes: Gentle stretch; stop if leg symptoms worsen.

name: Glute Bridge
primary_cues: Press through heels; lift hips until you feel glutes working.
Keep ribs down and avoid over-arching the low back.
progressions: Single-leg bridge (only if stable and pain-free)
dosage_notes: Hip strengthening can reduce load sensitivity and improves overall trunk-hip coordination.
range_of_motion_notes: Pain-free range; avoid end-range lumbar extension.

name: Dead Bug (Core Control)
primary_cues: Keep ribs down and low back gently supported (neutral).
Move slowly; stop before you lose control or arch.
Exhale as you extend a limb.
progressions: Extend both limbs (harder) only if stable
dosage_notes: Motor control / stabilisation exercises are commonly recommended as part of exercise therapy for LBP.
range_of_motion_notes: Keep spine neutral; small controlled motion.

name: Bird Dog
primary_cues: Keep hips level; reach long rather than high.
Brace gently; avoid rotation or sagging.
progressions: Add a small pause at end range
dosage_notes: Classic trunk coordination exercise for endurance and control.

name: Side Plank (Modified Knees)
primary_cues: Lift hips into a straight line from shoulders to knees.
Keep neck long; shoulder stacked over elbow.
progressions: Full side plank (feet) if strong and pain-free
regressions: Keep top hand on floor for balance
range_of_motion_notes: Isometric; stop if shoulder pain dominates.

name: Pallof Press (Anti-Rotation)
primary_cues: Stand tall; press band straight out and resist rotation.
Keep ribs down; avoid twisting through the torso.
dosage_notes: Strengthens trunk control in functional standing positions.

name: Hip Hinge Drill (Dowel / Hands-on Hips)
primary_cues: Send hips back while keeping spine long and neutral.
Bend knees slightly; feel load in hips/glutes, not the low back.
common_mistakes: Turning it into a squat (knees forward) unintentionally
progressions: Add light load (backpack) once pattern is solid
regressions: Reduce range (hinge to a wall target)
Hands slide along thighs to guide motion
dosage_notes: Movement retraining can improve confidence and reduce load spikes during daily lifting tasks.

name: Sit-to-Stand (Chair)
primary_cues: Lean slightly forward, push through feet, stand tall.
Control the way down; keep breathing.
dosage_notes: Functional leg/hip strength supports spine load tolerance in daily life.
range_of_motion_notes: Use pain-free range; adjust chair height.

name: Farmer Carry (Light Load)
primary_cues: Stand tall; ribs down; walk slowly with controlled steps.
Choose a load that feels stable and does not spike symptoms.
common_mistakes: Using a load that’s too heavy and bracing excessively
dosage_notes: Loaded carries are a practical strength/endurance option once baseline tolerance is established.

name: Walking (Progressive Plan)
primary_cues: Start with a duration that feels manageable and build gradually.
If a walk increases symptoms beyond your threshold or causes next-day flare, reduce and rebuild.
common_mistakes: Jumping from zero to long walks immediately
progressions: Increase total minutes per week gradually
Aim toward 30 minutes most days (as tolerated)
dosage_notes: Walking is low-cost and accessible; a walking + education program reduced recurrence risk in a large RCT (WalkBack).

name: Gentle Yoga Flow (Optional)
primary_cues: Move slowly and stay within comfortable ranges.
Skip any pose that increases symptoms sharply.
common_mistakes: Pushing into end ranges to “stretch it out”
dosage_notes: Mind–body exercise (e.g., yoga) can be an option for some people with chronic low back pain; evidence certainty varies.
range_of_motion_notes: Avoid forced end-range lumbar flexion/extension if irritable.
